Summary: Systematic: the passages present a logical role→mechanism→outcome description of PAX3-FOXO1. In ACP mice, PAX3-FOXO1 expression reprogrammed aP2-Cre labeled cells with muscle stem cell properties capable of muscle regeneration. In alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma, chromosomal translocations t(2;13) or t(1;13) generate the PAX3-FOXO1 fusion oncoprotein by joining the PAX3 DNA-binding domain with the FOXO1 transactivation domain. The presence of PAX3-FOXO1 foretells a worse prognosis and defines fusion-positive rhabdomyosarcoma. Alveolar tumors lacking PAX3/7-FOXO1 molecularly and clinically resemble embryonal RMS and are categorized as fusion-negative RMS. To test whether PAX3-FOXO1 could drive transformation into fusion-positive RMS, ACP mice were observed for tumor development and all 24 mice developed tumors with a median tumor-free survival of 251 days. The tumors did not include fusion-positive RMS and instead comprised diverse tumor types including mononuclear phagocytic sarcoma and angiosarcoma. Previously, aP2-Cre;Cdkn2a Flox mice developed only mononuclear phagocytic sarcoma, suggesting PAX3-FOXO1 drives diversity in the ACP tumor spectrum. Pax3-Foxo1 was validated in three ACP tumors and none of those tumors expressed Myod1 or Myog diagnostic for rhabdomyosarcoma.
Reference: Searcy, M. B., Larsen, R. K., Stevens, B. T., Zhang, Y., Jin, H., Drummond, C. J., Langdon, C. G., Gadek, K. E., Vuong, K., Reed, K. B., Garcia, M. R., Xu, B., Kimbrough, D. W., Adkins, G. PAX3-FOXO1 dictates myogenic reprogramming and rhabdomyosarcoma identity in endothelial progenitors. Nat Commun, 14(1), 7291. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41467-023-43044-1

Summary: A t(2;13)(q35;q14) translocation produces the chimeric oncogenic transcription factor PAX3-FOXO1, which is a hallmark of aggressive alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma. PAX3-FOXO1 cooperates with additional molecular changes to promote oncogenic transformation and tumorigenesis in various human and murine models. Its expression is generally restricted to RMS tumor cells, thus providing a very specific target for therapeutic approaches.
Reference: Nguyen, T. H., & Barr, F. G. (2018). Therapeutic Approaches Targeting PAX3-FOXO1 and Its Regulatory and Transcriptional Pathways in Rhabdomyosarcoma. Molecules, 23(11), E2798. https://doi.org/10.3390/molecules23112798

Summary: In rhabdomyosarcoma, the most common pediatric soft tissue sarcoma, a subset of tumors is fusion-positive due to PAX3-FOXO1, which fuses the DNA-binding domain of PAX3 with the transactivation domain of FOXO1. Fusion-positive tumors, especially those expressing PAX3-FOXO1, are higher-risk and more likely to be metastatic at diagnosis. PAX3-FOXO1 expression correlates with worse outcomes for localized and metastatic disease, with five-year overall survival as low as 13% versus 62% for fusion-negative disease.
Reference: Paras, K. I., Brunner, J. S., Montero, A. M., Boyer, J. A., Jackson, B. T., Chakraborty, S., Xie, A., Guillan, K., Siddiquee, A., Pajuelo, T. o. r. r. e. s. L., Rabinowitz, J. D., Kung, A. L., You, D., Dela, C. r. u. z. F. S., & Finley, L. W. S. (2025). PAX3-FOXO1 Drives Targetable Cell State-Dependent Metabolic Vulnerabilities in Rhabdomyosarcoma. Cancer Res, 85(23), 4718-4733. https://doi.org/10.1158/0008-5472.can-25-0315

Summary: In alveolar rhabdomyosarcoma (fusion-positive RMS; FP-RMS) a chromosomal translocation t(2;13)(q35;q14) produces an in-frame fusion of PAX3 and FOXO1 that defines most cases. PAX3-FOXO1 contains the N-terminal paired-box and homeobox DNA-binding domains of PAX3 fused to the C-terminal transactivation domain of FOXO1 and thereby alters the transcriptional program. FP-RMS cells are addicted to this fusion protein. PAX3-FOXO1 (P3F), as a transcription factor, is a notoriously challenging drug target. Indirect targeting strategies might be applied as an alternative to treat FP-RMS tumors.

Summary:
Relationship structure: causal chain.

PAX3-FOXO1 is an oncogenic fusion transcription factor that characterizes alveolar (fusion-positive) rhabdomyosarcoma and arises from recurrent chromosomal translocations. Expression of PAX3-FOXO1 is crucial for the survival and proliferation of fusion-positive RMS. ChIP-seq showed PAX3-FOXO1 highly binds a super-enhancer within the ALK locus and that ALK super-enhancer was used as a luciferase reporter of PAX3-FOXO1-driven transcription.
